title: Hotel / Airbnb Booking — System Design
description: Design a lodging reservation system: search available stays by location and date range, then book one without ever double-booking. The themes are date-range inventory, browse-vs-book consistency (approximate search but authoritative booking), the hold-then-confirm flow, and preventing double-booking under concurrency.

nodes.label: Guest Client
nodes.description: Searches stays by date/location, holds, and books.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes search, availability, and booking requests.

nodes.label: Search Service
nodes.description: Finds properties matching location, dates, filters.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Geo + attribute index of properties for discovery.

nodes.label: Availability Service
nodes.description: Authoritative answer to 'is this room free for these dates?'

nodes.label: Availability Store
nodes.description: Per-property per-date inventory; the source of truth for booking.

nodes.label: Booking Store
nodes.description: Authoritative reservations and their lifecycle state.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Authorizes during hold, captures on confirm.

nodes.label: Hold Reaper
nodes.description: Expires holds whose checkout didn't complete, releasing dates.

functional: Search available properties by location, date range, and filters.
Show a property's calendar / availability for chosen dates.
Hold dates during checkout, then confirm a booking with payment.
Cancel a booking and free the dates.
Prevent double-booking the same room for overlapping dates.
nonFunctional: Browse is read-heavy and can be approximately fresh (fast search).
Booking must be strongly consistent: never sell the same room twice for overlapping dates.
Low search latency; booking can be a touch slower but must be correct.
Scale to millions of properties and high search volume.
Hold + payment flow is reliable: an abandoned checkout releases inventory.

capacity.label: Hold TTL
capacity.note: Long enough to pay, short enough to free abandoned holds.

capacity.label: Consistency
capacity.note: Approximate on browse, exact at booking.

api.description: Search available stays by location, dates, and filters (approximate availability).
Hold a room for date range during checkout; authorizes payment.
Confirm a held booking; captures payment and finalizes the reservation.

dataModel.name: availability
dataModel.note: Per-property per-date inventory — the source of truth for booking.

patterns.name: Date-range inventory
patterns.what: Model availability per property per night so a stay is a contiguous range of open nights.

patterns.name: Approximate browse vs authoritative book
patterns.what: Search from a fast (slightly stale) availability cache; verify exactly at booking time.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy discovery where the book step must be exact.

patterns.name: Hold (reservation lock)
patterns.what: Temporarily mark the date range held during checkout so two guests can't both pay.

patterns.name: Atomic range reservation
patterns.what: Check-and-mark the whole date range in one atomic operation to prevent double-booking.
patterns.whenToUse: Concurrent bookings on the same room.

patterns.name: TTL hold expiry (reaping)
patterns.what: Auto-release holds whose checkout didn't complete, returning dates to inventory.
patterns.whenToUse: Holds that must not lock inventory forever.

patterns.name: Idempotent confirm
patterns.what: An idempotency key makes confirming a booking safe to retry without double-booking/charging.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving confirm steps over unreliable networks.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Check Availability, Then Write a Booking (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest booking flow: when a guest books, read the availability for the dates, and if it looks free, write a booking row. Search is a query over the same availability/property tables.

It reads naturally and is wrong under concurrency. Between the check and the write, a second guest can run the same check, see the same dates free, and also write a booking — a classic read-then-write race that double-books the room for overlapping dates. Separately, searching the authoritative availability tables on every browse doesn't scale to millions of properties and browse-heavy traffic.

So the baseline motivates the design: model per-night date-range inventory, reserve a range atomically (so exactly one booking wins), and serve browse from a fast approximate index/cache while the book step verifies the truth.

view.caption: The guest client calls the API gateway and booking service, which checks and reserves dates through the availability service against the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two guests check the same room for the same dates at the same moment, both see it free, and both write a booking. What did you just do?
whyNow: Check-then-write is the intuitive booking flow; watching it double-book under a concurrent race is exactly what motivates atomic range reservation and the approximate-browse / exact-book split.

recap.after: Bookings work by checking availability then writing a booking row; search hits the same tables.
recap.newRisk: The check-then-write gap double-books under concurrency, and searching the truth store on every browse doesn't scale.

traps.trap: Checking availability and then writing the booking in two separate steps.
traps.why: Two concurrent requests both pass the check before either writes, double-booking the room for overlapping dates.
traps.instead: Reserve the whole date range in one atomic conditional operation so exactly one booking wins.

steps.title: 2. Model Inventory as Date-Range Availability
description: Lodging inventory is per-night: a property/room is available or not for each date. A stay is a contiguous range of nights, and booking it means every night in the range is open.
Store availability per (property, date) with a state (open/held/booked). This per-night model is what makes overlapping-range conflict detection precise — two stays conflict iff they share any night.
This availability store is the source of truth for booking; everything browse-side is derived from it.

view.caption: The guest client queries the availability service for exact PDP availability, which reads and atomically reserves date ranges in the availability store.

options.name: Per-night availability rows (default)
options.description: Availability is stored as one row per (property, date) carrying an open/held/booked state, so a stay reserves every night row in its range.
pros: Two stays conflict iff they share any night, so overlap detection is exact and simple.
A row per (property, date) maps cleanly onto atomic conditional updates over a date range.
Natural state machine per night (open/held/booked) for holds, reaper release, and pricing per date.
cons: A long stay touches many rows, so reserve/release operate on a range, not a single row.
Row count is properties x calendar horizon — large, though bounded and shardable by property.
Open-ended or far-future inventory needs a bounded horizon or lazy row creation.

view.caption: The availability service reserves against per-night rows in the availability store, one row per date.

options.name: Interval / range records per booking
options.description: Each reservation is stored as a single (start, end) interval record, and conflicts are found with an interval-overlap query rather than per-night rows.
pros: One row per reservation (start, end) is compact — no row-per-night explosion.
Matches how a stay is conceived: a single contiguous interval.
Cheap to store for sparse, far-future inventory.
cons: Conflict detection is an interval-overlap query, which needs careful indexing to stay fast and correct.
Atomic 'reserve this range' is harder to express than a conditional update on fixed night rows.
Per-night state (held vs booked, per-date price) is awkward to attach to an interval model.

view.caption: The availability service reserves against interval records in the availability store, one range per booking.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why model availability per night rather than per booking?

concepts.definition: Per-night availability; a stay reserves a contiguous range of open nights.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes overlapping-stay conflicts exact and range reservation atomic.
concepts.example: Jun 1-4 books nights 1,2,3; conflicts with any stay touching those nights.

whyNow: The per-night inventory model is the foundation; search, holds, and double-book prevention all reason about date ranges over it.

recap.after: Per-night availability is the source of truth.
recap.newRisk: Searching this directly across millions of properties on every browse is too slow.

steps.title: 3. Search: Approximate Browse
description: Browse is read-heavy and tolerant of slight staleness. Build a search index over properties (geo + attributes) and an availability read cache; search returns properties that appear available for the dates, fast.

Search availability is approximate by design — a property shown as available might get booked by someone else a second later. That's acceptable for browse; the booking step is where exactness is enforced.
Separating fast/approximate discovery from slow/exact booking is the key consistency split: don't make every search hit the authoritative availability store.

view.caption: The guest client searches through the gateway and search service, which queries the search index by geo and filters and reads approximate availability from the read cache.

options.name: Search index + availability read cache (default)
options.description: Browse hits a geo+attribute search index plus an availability read cache fed from booking changes, keeping the authoritative store out of the read path.
pros: Browse is fast: geo + attribute queries hit the index, approximate availability comes from the cache.
Read-heavy browse traffic never touches the authoritative availability store, which stays free for bookings.
Index and cache scale and replicate independently of the booking core.
cons: Search availability is approximate — a property shown free may be booked a second later (corrected at the booking step).
The availability cache must be fed from booking changes, adding a freshness pipeline.
Two derived stores (index + cache) to keep reasonably fresh.

view.caption: The search service queries the search index and reads approximate availability from a read cache, keeping browse fast and eventually consistent.

options.name: Query the authoritative availability store directly
options.description: Search queries the authoritative availability store directly, so browse runs filters and geo lookups against the same transactional rows that bookings write.
pros: Search results are always exact — never shows a night that is actually taken.
No derived index or cache to build, feed, or keep fresh.
Simplest possible architecture: one source of truth for both browse and booking.
cons: Browse-heavy traffic hammers the booking-critical store, risking contention with real reservations.
Geo + multi-attribute filtering over millions of properties is slow on a transactional store.
Couples read scaling to the write store, the exact thing the design wants to separate.

view.caption: The search service queries the authoritative availability store directly for exact availability on every browse.

options.name: Event-driven materialized search view
options.description: Availability changes stream into a dedicated denormalized browse view that a projector keeps current, so search reads the materialized view rather than the source.
pros: Booking/availability changes stream into a dedicated browse view, so search reflects writes within seconds.
Decouples the read shape (denormalized, geo-indexed) from the write shape entirely.
Rebuildable: a corrupted view is reprojected from the authoritative store.
cons: Eventually consistent — still approximate at the moment of search, just fresher.
Adds a change stream + projector to build and operate.
More moving parts than a cache for a property set that may not change that fast.

view.caption: Availability changes flow from the store through a change stream into the search index, which the search service queries for browse.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should search read the authoritative availability store on every query?

concepts.definition: Discovery uses a fast cache; correctness is enforced only at booking.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps browse fast and cheap without risking double-booking (verified later).
concepts.example: Search shows ~available results; the hold step re-checks authoritatively.

whyNow: The browse-vs-book consistency split is the central design insight; it lets search scale while booking stays correct.

recap.after: Fast approximate search; truth reserved for booking.
recap.newRisk: Two guests can both reach checkout for the same room — booking must not double-sell.

steps.title: 4. Hold-Then-Confirm Checkout
description: Booking is multi-step (review, pay), during which someone else mustn't grab the room. When checkout starts, place a hold on the date range: mark those nights 'held' with a short TTL and authorize payment.
The hold gives the guest a window to complete payment without the dates being sold from under them. If they confirm, the hold becomes a booking; if they abandon, the hold expires.
Holds are short (minutes) so abandoned checkouts don't lock inventory — a balance between giving guests time and not starving real demand.

view.caption: The booking service reserves dates via the availability service, places a TTL hold in the hold store, then authorizes payment through the payment service.

options.name: Explicit hold with TTL (default)
options.description: Starting checkout marks the date range 'held' with a short TTL and authorizes payment; confirming turns the hold into a booking, abandonment lets it expire.
pros: Marks the nights 'held' for a few minutes so no one else can grab them during payment.
Abandoned checkouts self-heal: the hold expires and the reaper releases the nights.
Clear lifecycle (open -> held -> booked) the rest of the system can reason about.
cons: Needs a hold store and a reaper to expire abandoned holds.
Choosing the TTL is a tradeoff: too short rushes guests, too long starves real demand.
Held-but-never-confirmed inventory is briefly unsellable.

view.caption: The booking service places an explicit TTL hold in the hold store after reserving dates and before capturing payment.

options.name: No hold — reserve atomically at confirm
options.description: There is no held state — nights stay open until confirm, when the booking service atomically reserves the whole range as the final step of checkout.
pros: Simpler: no hold store, no TTL, no reaper — nights are only ever open or booked.
Inventory is never tied up by someone who is merely 'thinking about it'.
Fewer moving parts and states to operate.
cons: A guest can complete payment only to find the room was booked out from under them mid-checkout.
Wasted payment authorizations that must be voided when the final reserve fails.
Bad UX for popular rooms exactly when contention is highest.

view.caption: The booking service authorizes payment first, then atomically reserves dates in the availability store at confirm with no hold.

options.name: Pessimistic lock for the checkout window
options.description: The booking service takes a database lock on the date-range rows and keeps it for the whole payment window, blocking any other booker until checkout finishes.
pros: Holding a lock on the date range guarantees exclusivity while the guest pays.
No 'held' state to model — the lock itself blocks other bookers.
Conceptually simple exclusivity.
cons: A lock held for minutes across a slow human payment step ties up a DB resource the whole time.
A client that abandons or crashes mid-checkout strands the lock until a timeout — same expiry problem as a hold, with worse blast radius.
Long-held locks hurt throughput and can cascade under contention on hot properties.

view.caption: The booking service takes a row lock on the date range in the availability store for the duration of the checkout window.

steps.decisionPrompt: A guest is on the payment screen for 3 minutes. How do you stop someone else booking the same room meanwhile?

concepts.definition: A short TTL mark on the date range during checkout so it can't be double-sold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects the inventory across a multi-step, human-paced checkout.
concepts.example: Mark Jun 1-4 held for 10 min; confirm -> booked; expire -> open.

whyNow: The hold is how a multi-step checkout coexists with contended inventory; it's the booking-flow backbone.

recap.after: Date range held with a TTL during payment.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent holds on the same range must not both succeed — need atomic range reservation.

traps.why: Abandoned checkouts lock rooms forever, starving real bookings.
traps.instead: Short TTL holds reaped on expiry.

steps.title: 5. Preventing Double-Booking
description: The core correctness requirement: never sell the same room twice for overlapping dates. The hold acquisition must be atomic — check that every night in the range is open and mark them held in one operation, so two concurrent requests can't both pass the check.

Use a conditional/transactional update over the date range (or optimistic concurrency with the version column): the first request wins the range; the second sees a conflict and is rejected. The availability store, sharded by property, serializes writes per property.
This is the same check-and-reserve-atomically discipline as any limited-inventory system, applied to a contiguous date range rather than a single counter.

view.caption: The booking service reserves dates through the availability service, which performs an atomic date-range reserve against the availability store to prevent double-booking.

options.name: Atomic conditional update / OCC (default)
options.description: A single conditional/transactional update checks every night is open and marks them held at once, using a version column so the losing concurrent request just fails.
pros: Check-all-nights-open-and-mark-held in one conditional update, so two concurrent requests can't both pass.
Optimistic concurrency (version column) needs no long-held locks — the loser just retries or fails.
Sharding availability by property serializes writes per property naturally.
cons: The loser of a race must handle a conflict and retry, adding client-side complexity.
Hot dates on a famous property serialize on one property's rows — correct but a throughput ceiling.
Requires the store to support the conditional/transactional primitive over a range.

view.caption: The availability service uses an atomic conditional update (optimistic concurrency) on the availability store so only one booking wins a contested range.

options.name: Pessimistic row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE)
options.description: The transaction does SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the night rows before reserving, so the second request blocks and then sees the nights already taken.
pros: Locking the night rows before reserving guarantees exactly one writer wins, no retry loop.
Familiar transactional pattern on a relational availability store.
Deterministic: the second request blocks, then sees the nights taken.
cons: Locks held under load can queue and degrade throughput on hot properties.
Risk of deadlocks when ranges are locked in different orders across requests.
Doesn't extend cleanly to a sharded/distributed store the way a conditional update does.

view.caption: The availability service takes a pessimistic SELECT ... FOR UPDATE row lock on the availability store before reserving.

options.name: Distributed lock per property+range
options.description: A separate lock service (Redis/ZooKeeper) grants an exclusive lock on property+range, coordinating mutual exclusion outside the availability store's own writes.
pros: A lock service (e.g. Redis/ZooKeeper) coordinates exclusivity even across multiple booking services.
Works when the availability store itself can't offer a strong conditional primitive.
Decouples mutual exclusion from the storage engine.
cons: Introduces a separate, critical dependency that must itself be HA — a new SPOF if it falls over.
Lock-then-write isn't atomic with the DB write, so a crash between them can leak a lock or a phantom reserve.
Clock/lease expiry edge cases (lock lost early) can still allow a double-book without fencing tokens.

view.caption: The booking service first acquires a property+range lock from a distributed lock service before reserving through the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two guests submit a hold for the same room and dates at the same millisecond. How does exactly one win?

concepts.definition: Check all nights open and mark them held in a single atomic/conditional operation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents the read-then-write race that causes double-booking.
concepts.example: UPDATE availability SET state='held' WHERE property=p AND date IN range AND state='open' — succeeds only if all rows matched.

whyNow: Double-book prevention is the non-negotiable correctness property; atomic check-and-mark is the precise mechanism and the senior signal.

recap.after: Exactly one hold wins a contested range.
recap.newRisk: The confirm step (payment) can be retried or interrupted — it must be idempotent.

traps.trap: Check availability, then mark held in a separate step.
traps.why: Two requests both read 'open' before either marks held -> double-booking.
traps.instead: Atomic conditional update over the whole range.

flows.name: Atomic range reservation: exactly one hold wins
flows.note: Two concurrent holds hit the same range; the conditional update over all nights succeeds for exactly one, the other sees a conflict.

steps.title: 6. Confirm, Idempotency, and Expiry
description: On confirm, capture the authorized payment and transition the hold to a confirmed booking atomically (held nights -> booked). Use an idempotency key so a retried confirm (network blip) doesn't create two bookings or double-charge.
If the guest abandons checkout, the hold reaper expires it past its TTL and releases the nights back to 'open' — inventory self-heals without manual cleanup.

The booking lifecycle (held -> confirmed -> cancelled) is durable; cancellation frees the dates and refunds per policy. Confirm is the money-moving step, so correctness here mirrors the wallet/payment discipline.

view.caption: The booking service captures payment and persists the reservation to the booking store, while a hold reaper finds expired holds and releases their dates back to the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: The guest pays, but the confirm response is lost and they retry. How do you avoid a second booking/charge?

concepts.definition: A confirm keyed for dedup, plus a reaper that releases expired holds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes confirm retry-safe and ensures abandoned holds never permanently lock inventory.
concepts.example: Retry with key k_1 returns the original booking; an unpaid hold expires and frees its nights.

whyNow: Idempotent confirm + reaping complete the correctness story: no double-booking, no double-charge, no leaked inventory.

recap.before: Confirm could double-book/charge; holds could leak.
recap.newRisk: Hot properties (popular listing, event dates) concentrate contention; search freshness lags truth.

failureDrills.scenario: Booking service crashes after capturing payment but before recording the booking.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Retry with the idempotency key recognizes the captured payment and finalizes one booking.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent confirm keyed to the hold/payment.

flows.name: Idempotent confirm: capture payment, finalize once
flows.note: Confirm captures the authorized payment and transitions held nights to booked under an idempotency key; a retried confirm returns the same booking.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Search, Hot Properties, and Freshness
description: Shard the availability and booking stores by property id so per-property writes serialize locally and load spreads. Search scales separately: the index + availability cache are derived and replicated for browse volume.

Hot dates/properties (a festival weekend, a famous listing) concentrate booking contention on one property's rows; this is bounded — contention is per-property, and the atomic range reservation keeps it correct, just serialized.

Keep search freshness reasonable by updating the availability cache from booking changes (a booking removes those nights from browse soon after). Stale search is acceptable because booking re-checks the truth — the cache only needs to be approximately right.

view.caption: The search service reads approximate availability from a cache refreshed by the availability store, keeping browse fast while bookings persist to the booking store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A famous listing's peak weekend gets thousands of concurrent booking attempts. What stays correct, and what's the bottleneck?
whyNow: Closing on sharding + hot properties + cache freshness shows the browse/book split scales and stays correct under contention.

recap.after: Property-sharded truth; replicated approximate search; cache updated from bookings.
recap.newRisk: Search-vs-truth lag and per-property hot rows — bounded by re-check-at-book and per-property serialization.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-property sharding serializes its writes; atomic range reservation keeps correctness; show 'just booked' fast via cache update.
Update the cache from booking events; the book step re-verifies authoritatively.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Lodging Reservation System
finalDesign.description: Availability is modeled per property per night (the source of truth). Browse is served from a fast, approximate search index + availability cache; the booking flow enforces exactness. Starting checkout places a TTL hold via an atomic range reservation (check-all-nights-open-and-mark-held in one operation), so concurrent attempts can't double-book; payment is authorized at hold and captured on an idempotent confirm that transitions held nights to booked. A reaper releases abandoned holds. Availability/booking stores shard by property (serializing per-property writes); the search cache updates from booking events while the book step always re-checks the truth.

view.caption: The guest client reaches the gateway for search over the index and availability cache, and booking, where the booking service reserves via the availability store, places holds, pays, and persists to the booking store while a reaper releases expired holds.

functional.how: TTL hold + idempotent confirm with payment.

say: Confirm date-range inventory and the browse(approx)-vs-book(exact) split.
Pin no-double-booking as the hard correctness requirement.
Note browse is read-heavy; booking is comparatively rare but must be exact.
Per-night availability as the source of truth.
Fast approximate search from an index + cache.
Atomic range reservation to prevent double-booking.
Property sharding, hot dates, and search freshness.
Tradeoffs: hold TTL length, search staleness vs truth, per-property contention.
Mention overbooking strategies and multi-room properties as extensions.

expectations: Models per-night availability and books it.
May miss the atomic reservation race and browse/book split.
Designs approximate browse vs exact book.
Reasons about per-property contention and sharding.
Discusses overbooking policy and hold-TTL tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you intentionally overbook (like airlines) and manage the risk?
How do you keep search availability fresh without hammering the truth store?
How long should a hold TTL be, and what's the tradeoff?
How would you handle a property with multiple identical rooms (inventory > 1 per night)?
How do you prevent a hot listing's peak dates from becoming a write hotspot?
